section.title: The Lord Is My Shepherd
section.sectionRationale: The best known and most beloved psalm in the Psalter, David's picture of the LORD as shepherd shifts midway from speaking about God, 'He', to speaking directly to Him, 'You', at the moment of deepest danger, the valley of the shadow of death. Its imagery of green pastures, a shepherd's rod and staff, a prepared table, and dwelling forever in God's house is fulfilled in Jesus, who names Himself the good shepherd in John 10.
section.thesis: David's confidence that the LORD is his shepherd, providing, guiding, and protecting him even through the valley of the shadow of death, is fulfilled completely in Jesus Christ, the good shepherd who lays down His life for His sheep.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the shepherd's provision in green pastures and quiet waters; the shift to direct address in the valley of the shadow of death, fearless because You are with me; and the closing picture of a prepared table and a home in the LORD's house forever.

verses.text: The LORD is my {{roeh|shepherd}}; I shall not want.
He makes me lie down in green pastures; He leads me beside quiet waters.

He restores my soul; He guides me in the paths of righteousness 
for the sake of His name.

units.label: I will fear no evil, for You are with me
units.note: the shift to direct address in the valley

verses.text: Even though I walk through the valley of the shadow of death, I will fear no evil, for You are with me; Your rod and Your staff, they comfort me.

units.label: I will dwell in the house of the Lord forever
units.note: a prepared table and an everlasting home

verses.text: You prepare a table before me in the presence of my enemies. You anoint my head with oil; my cup overflows.
Surely goodness and {{chesed|mercy}} will follow me all the days of my life, and I will dwell in the house of the LORD forever.

roeh.gloss: Shepherd, one who tends, feeds, and guides a flock. The image draws on David's own early life tending sheep and on Israel's long tradition of describing God, and Israel's kings, as shepherds of the people, fulfilled completely in Jesus, the good shepherd, John 10:11.

chesed.gloss: Steadfast, covenant loving devotion, rendered here 'mercy' or 'lovingkindness.' David pictures this loyal love as actively pursuing him, following him, all the days of his life, not a distant attitude but an active, pursuing presence.

basic.context: David pictures God as a shepherd who provides everything he needs: green pastures, quiet waters, a restored soul, guidance on right paths. At the psalm's very center, the moment of greatest danger, the valley of the shadow of death, David's language shifts from talking about God, 'He leads me', to talking directly to Him, 'You are with me'. The psalm closes with a picture of a table prepared even in the presence of enemies, an anointed and overflowing welcome, and the settled hope of dwelling in God's house forever.
basic.hermeneutics: This psalm's central turn, from third person description to direct, personal address exactly when danger is named, teaches something profound about faith: nearness to God is often found and felt most intensely not before or after hardship but in the very middle of it. The whole psalm should be read as personal, devotional truth meant to be prayed, not merely admired as poetry.
basic.originalLanguages: David calls the LORD his ro'eh, shepherd, an image rooted in his own early life tending sheep and in Israel's long tradition of shepherd kings. His closing confidence rests in chesed, God's covenant loving devotion, pictured here as actively pursuing him, following him, all the days of his life.
basic.typology: David's LORD as shepherd anticipates Jesus, who explicitly claims this title for Himself, the good shepherd, John 10:11, who, unlike a hired hand, lays down His life for the sheep, fulfilling and surpassing every promise of provision and protection this psalm describes.
basic.christ: Jesus directly claims the title this psalm gives to the LORD: 'I am the good shepherd. The good shepherd lays down his life for the sheep', John 10:11. Every provision David describes, rest, restoration, guided paths, fearless presence in the valley of death, a prepared table, an everlasting home, finds its fullest and final meaning in Christ, who does not merely lead sheep beside quiet waters but walks through death itself ahead of His flock and rises to prepare an eternal home for them, John 14:2-3.
basic.god: God is shown as intimately, personally involved in provision and protection, a shepherd who knows His sheep individually, and whose presence, not the absence of danger, is what removes fear even in the valley of death's shadow.
basic.crossReferences: John 10:11-15 shows Jesus claiming the title of good shepherd, laying down His life for the sheep. Ezekiel 34:11-16 pictures God Himself searching for and shepherding His scattered people. Revelation 7:17 pictures the Lamb as shepherd, guiding His people to springs of living water.
basic.oneStory: David's confidence in the LORD as his shepherd, sufficient through green pastures and the valley of death alike, finds its complete fulfillment in Jesus Christ, the good shepherd who laid down His life for His sheep and now leads all who trust Him toward an everlasting home in the Father's house.

context.literary: A psalm of trust built on an extended shepherd metaphor in verses 1-4 that transitions, at the psalm's structural and emotional center, into a host or banquet metaphor in verses 5-6, unified by the consistent theme of God's personal, sufficient provision and the striking shift from third person to direct second person address exactly at the point of greatest danger, verse 4.
context.historical: Attributed to David, who spent his early years as a literal shepherd before becoming king, giving this image both personal authenticity and connection to the ancient Near Eastern convention of describing kings and gods as shepherds of their people.
context.canonical: Among the most frequently referenced psalms in both Jewish and Christian devotional tradition, its shepherd imagery developed further in Ezekiel 34's prophecy of God shepherding His scattered flock and fulfilled explicitly by Jesus's self-identification as the good shepherd in John 10.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To express settled, personal confidence in God's complete provision, guidance, and protection through every circumstance of life, including the direct experience of mortal danger, grounded in a relationship intimate enough to shift from description to direct address.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes David's own experience and confidence; it functions prescriptively as a model prayer of trust available to every believer, teaching that fearlessness in danger comes not from the absence of the valley but from the shepherd's presence within it.

debates.question: Does 'the valley of the shadow of death' in verse 4 refer specifically to the experience of dying, or more broadly to any deep danger or distress?

views.label: The phrase refers broadly to any deep, dark valley of danger or distress in life, using death imagery poetically to describe the darkest kind of trouble, not necessarily the literal moment of dying
views.case: The Hebrew phrase can be rendered 'deep darkness' or 'deep shadow' more generally, and the psalm's overall shepherding imagery, guiding sheep through dangerous terrain, fits a broader range of life's darkest valleys, not death alone.

views.label: The phrase specifically and primarily anticipates the experience of dying itself, since the following verses describe an even more secure, everlasting home in God's presence, suggesting the psalm's scope extends through death and beyond it
views.case: The psalm's closing hope of dwelling in the LORD's house forever suggests its scope was never meant to be limited only to temporary troubles in this life, but to include confidence even facing death itself, a hope confirmed by later, fuller biblical revelation of resurrection.

debates.bearing: Both readings affirm the psalm's central point, that God's presence removes fear regardless of the danger's severity; whether the valley refers to any dark trouble or to death specifically, the shepherd's presence, not the absence of danger, is what grounds fearless confidence, a truth fully realized in Christ who walked through actual death and rose again.

study.originalLanguages: David's title for God, ro'eh, shepherd, draws on his own early life tending sheep, and his closing confidence rests on chesed, God's covenant loving devotion, pictured as actively pursuing him all his days rather than a distant or passive attitude.

typology.type: The LORD as shepherd who provides, guides, and protects, verses 1-4
typology.fulfillment: Jesus Christ, who explicitly names Himself the good shepherd who lays down His life for the sheep, John 10:11.

typology.type: A table prepared and an everlasting home in the LORD's house, verses 5-6
typology.fulfillment: The place Christ has gone to prepare for His people in His Father's house, John 14:2-3, and the eternal fellowship pictured at the marriage supper of the Lamb, Revelation 19:9.

study.christ: Jesus takes up this psalm's central title directly, declaring, 'I am the good shepherd. The good shepherd lays down his life for the sheep', John 10:11. Every element of David's confidence is fulfilled and surpassed in Him: He leads His people to true rest, restores souls through the new birth, walks with His people through the valley of the shadow of death, having Himself passed through death and conquered it, and prepares not merely a table but an everlasting home in His Father's house for all who trust Him, John 14:2-3.
study.god: God is revealed here as intimately, personally involved, a shepherd who knows and provides for His sheep individually rather than a distant ruler, and whose presence, not the removal of danger, is what makes fearlessness possible even in the valley of death's shadow.

crossReferences.note: Jesus explicitly claims the title good shepherd, laying down His life for the sheep, fulfilling this psalm's central image.
Pictures God Himself searching for and shepherding His scattered, wounded, and lost sheep, developing this psalm's imagery further.
Pictures the Lamb as shepherd, guiding His people to springs of living water and wiping away every tear, an eternal fulfillment of this psalm's hope.

study.oneStory: David's confidence that the LORD is his shepherd, sufficient through green pastures and the valley of death's shadow alike, finds its complete and final fulfillment in Jesus Christ, the good shepherd who laid down His life for His sheep and rose again to prepare an everlasting home for all who follow Him.

sermon.title: The Shepherd, the Host, and the Highway Patrol
sermon.why: Piper walks through this entire psalm's picture of God as shepherd, host, and pursuing goodness, showing that God leads His people in righteousness for His own name's sake and that He Himself, not merely His gifts, is the fulfillment of all our longings.

quiz.q: What striking shift happens in verse 4?

options.text: David moves from speaking about God in the third person, 'He', to speaking directly to God in the second person, 'You', exactly at the point of greatest danger.
options.note: This grammatical shift at the psalm's structural center is one of its most theologically significant features.

options.text: David switches from Hebrew poetry to prose narrative.
options.note: The psalm remains poetic throughout; the notable shift is in grammatical person, not literary form.

options.text: The psalm changes speakers from David to an unnamed narrator.
options.note: The psalm maintains a single first person speaker throughout; the shift is in how that speaker addresses God, not a change of speaker.

options.text: David begins speaking about his enemies instead of about God.
options.note: While enemies are mentioned in verse 5, the key shift identified in verse 4 is from describing God in third person to addressing Him directly in second person.

quiz.q: What does David say gives him fearlessness in the valley of the shadow of death?

options.text: God's presence with him, not the absence of danger.
options.note: David explicitly says 'I will fear no evil, for You are with me,' locating his confidence in God's presence rather than safety from danger.

options.text: His own physical strength and courage.
options.note: The text attributes his fearlessness to God's presence, not to David's own strength.

options.text: The complete absence of any real danger in the valley.
options.note: The valley is explicitly named as one of deep danger, the shadow of death; his confidence is despite the danger, not because it is absent.

options.text: A promise that the valley would end quickly.
options.note: The text does not emphasize the valley's brevity; it emphasizes God's abiding presence within it.

quiz.q: What is significant about the setting of the table in verse 5?

options.text: It is prepared in the very presence of David's enemies, showing God's provision and protection even amid ongoing opposition.
options.note: The table's setting 'in the presence of my enemies' highlights God's provision despite continued surrounding danger, not after all danger has passed.

options.text: It occurs only after all of David's enemies have been completely destroyed.
options.note: The text specifies the table is prepared in the presence of enemies, not after their destruction.

options.text: It takes place in a private location far from any enemies.
options.note: The text explicitly locates the table 'in the presence of my enemies,' not in isolation from them.

options.text: It represents a future heavenly banquet with no connection to present circumstances.
options.note: While it does anticipate future hope, the text explicitly ties it to David's present circumstance of surrounding enemies.

quiz.q: How does Jesus directly connect Himself to this psalm's central image?

options.text: He declares in John 10:11, 'I am the good shepherd. The good shepherd lays down his life for the sheep.'
options.note: This is a direct, explicit claim by Jesus to the very title and role this psalm assigns to the LORD.

options.text: He never references shepherd imagery anywhere in His teaching.
options.note: Jesus explicitly and repeatedly uses shepherd imagery, most notably in John 10.

options.text: He compares Himself only to the sheep, not to the shepherd.
options.note: Jesus identifies Himself as the shepherd, not merely as one of the sheep, in John 10:11.

options.text: He refers to Himself as a hired hand rather than the shepherd.
options.note: Jesus explicitly contrasts Himself with a hired hand, identifying Himself instead as the good shepherd who lays down His life.

quiz.q: What is the psalm's closing hope in verse 6?

options.text: That goodness and mercy will follow him all his days, and he will dwell in the LORD's house forever.
options.note: This is the psalm's settled, confident conclusion of ongoing pursuit by God's goodness and an everlasting home.

options.text: That his enemies will eventually forgive him.
options.note: The psalm's closing hope concerns God's goodness and mercy pursuing David, not his enemies' forgiveness.

options.text: That he will acquire significant material wealth in his lifetime.
options.note: The closing hope is relational and eternal, dwelling with God, not primarily material wealth.

options.text: That his descendants alone, not he himself, will dwell with God.
options.note: The text is personal and direct, 'I will dwell in the house of the LORD forever,' not limited to future descendants.

questions: David's language shifts from talking about God to talking directly to God exactly at the point of greatest danger. Where in your own hardest moments have you found yourself drawing nearer to God rather than further away, or where do you wish that had been true?

The psalm says fearlessness comes from God's presence with us, not the absence of danger. What 'valley' are you currently walking through, and how might focusing on God's presence within it, rather than waiting for it to end, change how you face it?

God prepares a table 'in the presence of my enemies', provision given amid ongoing opposition rather than after it ends. Where do you need to trust God's provision even while your difficulties or opponents are still present?

Jesus claims the title of good shepherd who lays down His life for the sheep. How does knowing that the shepherd of this psalm is not distant but gave His own life for you change the way you read David's confidence here?

The psalm closes with confidence that goodness and mercy will 'follow' him, actively pursue him, all his days. Looking back over your life, where can you specifically identify God's goodness and mercy having pursued you, even in seasons you did not recognize it at the time?

translationNotes.note: Shepherd renders ro'eh, an image rooted in David's own early life tending sheep before he became king, and in the ancient Near Eastern convention of describing rulers and deities as shepherds of their people.

Valley of the shadow of death translates a Hebrew phrase, tsalmaveth, that can also be rendered 'deep darkness' or 'deep shadow', broad enough to include both the literal experience of dying and any deeply dark and dangerous circumstance.
Mercy renders chesed, God's steadfast covenant loving devotion, pictured here with an active verb, follow, suggesting this love actively pursues the believer rather than remaining passive or distant.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture toolkit for Psalm 23:1-6. No em or en dashes present in the source text or in this file. Sermon link verified this session via web_fetch of desiringgod.org.
